672	\begin{lemma}
673	
674	\leanok
675	[Closure of \(\otimes\) on the \(\otimes\)-invertible carrier
676	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
677	  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
678	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjOnProduct}
679	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
680	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
681	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
682	  % NOTE: the Lean target `tensorObjOnProduct` provides ONLY the
683	  % operation-closure on the subtype (that the tensor product of two
684	  % `LineBundle.OnProduct` objects is again one). The remaining group-law
685	  % data stated in this block --- unit-membership of O_{C×T}, the
686	  % dual/inverse being a two-sided tensor inverse, and the
687	  % associativity/unit/commutativity existence-of-iso facts --- are SEPARATE
688	  % items, NOT delivered by `tensorObjOnProduct`. Under the
689	  % tensor-invertibility re-point the carrier `OnProduct` will be
690	  % re-instantiated as `{ M : (pullback πC πT).Modules // IsInvertible M }`
691	  % (def:scheme_modules_isinvertible); the coherence isos
692	  % (lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso)
693	  % and the units-of-monoid engine (lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup) supply
694	  % the group-law data, so none of these items is blocked on
695	  % \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. `IsLocallyTrivial` is retained as a
696	  % separate predicate, connected to `IsInvertible` only by an
697	  % off-critical-path equivalence.
698	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes. Under the planned re-point of
699	  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, the carrier is the

120	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
121	\]
122	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
123	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
124	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
125	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
126	
127	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
128	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
129	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
130	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
131	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
132	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
133	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
134	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
135	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
136	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
137	tensor-product operation
138	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
139	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
140	
141	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
142	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
143	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
144	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
145	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
146	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The data this operation must supply for the group law are the monoidal
147	\emph{coherence isomorphisms} of \(\otimes_X\) --- the associator
148	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), the left/right unitors
149	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}) and the braiding
150	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) --- each obtained by the cheap pattern
151	\(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) applied to the corresponding presheaf-level
152	isomorphism on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\), with no recourse to the
153	abstract \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\) adjoint. Together with the
154	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
155	(which carries the inverse existentially) these assemble the commutative monoid
156	of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units group
157	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The project does \emph{not} require a
158	global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance on all quasi-coherent
159	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure:
160	monoidal coherence beyond these three isomorphisms is never consumed by a group
161	law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}). The
162	open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
163	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is \emph{not} on this critical path and is
164	retained only as an optional supplement.
165	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
166	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
167	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
168	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
169	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
170	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
171	independently of any upstream timing.
172	
173	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
174	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
175	
176	\begin{definition}
177	
178	\leanok
179	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
180	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
181	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
182	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
183	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
184	  \[
185	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
186	  \]
187	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
188	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
189	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
190	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
191	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
192	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
193	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
194	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
195	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
196	  canonical compatibility
197	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
198	     \;\cong\;
199	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
200	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
201	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
202	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
203	
204	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
205	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
206	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
207	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
208	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
209	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
210	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
211	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
212	\end{definition}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}
215	
216	\leanok
217	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
218	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
219	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
220	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
221	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
222	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
223	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
224	  \[
225	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
226	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
227	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
228	  \]
229	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
230	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
231	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
232	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
233	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
234	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
235	  \[
236	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
237	    \qquad
238	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
239	  \]
240	  the associator
241	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
242	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
243	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
244	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they have no current Lean
245	  declaration and are off the critical path, recorded in
246	  \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
247	\end{lemma}
248	
249	\begin{proof}
250	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
251	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
252	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
253	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
254	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
255	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
256	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
257	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
258	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
259	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
260	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
261	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
262	\end{proof}
263	
264	\begin{remark}
265	[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
266	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
267	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
268	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
269	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
270	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
271	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
272	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
273	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
274	  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
275	  depend on it.
276	
277	  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
278	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
279	  classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
280	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
281	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
282	  coherence data of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
283	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never consumed by the group
284	  law. Conversely, building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all
285	  quasi-coherent modules would route through the localization-monoidal
286	  transport \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
287	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
288	  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
289	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
290	  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
291	  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
292	  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
293	  buys coherence the group law never uses.
294	
295	  The group law needs only the four existence-of-iso lemmas of
296	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}, each assembled from the
297	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. We
298	  therefore record the global monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the
299	  critical path} and build the group law directly on the line-bundle
300	  subcategory. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
301	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
302	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
303	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) over
304	  a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and no
305	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
306	\end{remark}
307	
308	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
309	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
310	
311	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
312	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
313	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
314	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
315	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section first isolates the one
316	genuinely-hard ingredient --- the restriction-compatibility
317	isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and then records the
318	\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
319	unit, commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law

320	consumes, each assembled from that ingredient on a common trivialising
321	cover, together with their restriction to the
322	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype and the interaction with the
323	pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). These four facts (\emph{not} a
324	\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance) are exactly the group-law data; cf.\
325	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
326	
327	\begin{lemma}
328	
329	\leanok
330	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
331	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
332	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
333	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
334	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
335	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
336	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
337	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
338	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
339	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
340	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
341	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
342	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
343	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
344	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
345	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
346	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
347	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
348	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
349	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
350	  \[
351	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
352	      \;\;:=\;\;
353	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
354	  \]
355	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
356	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
357	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
358	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
359	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
360	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
361	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
362	\end{lemma}
363	
364	\begin{proof}
365	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
366	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
367	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
368	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
369	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
370	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
371	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
372	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
373	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
374	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
375	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
376	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
377	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
378	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
379	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
380	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
381	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
382	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
383	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
384	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
385	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
386	
387	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
388	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
389	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
390	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
391	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
392	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
393	\end{proof}
394	
395	\begin{lemma}
396	
397	\leanok
398	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
399	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
400	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
401	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
402	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
403	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
404	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
405	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
406	  morphism
407	  \[
408	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
409	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
410	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
411	  \]
412	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
413	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
414	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
415	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
416	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
417	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products. The intended Lean
418	  target is
419	  \(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\).
420	\end{lemma}

423	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}

433	  % exist), and (H2) strong-monoidal `restrictScalars` along the ring iso
434	  % f.appIso (upgrade the file's lax `restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal` to strong;
435	  % bottoms out in the absent "restrictScalars along a ring iso is strong
436	  % monoidal"). Steps 1--2 (restrictFunctorIsoPullback, sheafificationCompPullback,
437	  % strip outer sheafification via mapIso) ARE formalized and GREEN. Step 3 and
438	  % the closing "30--60 line helper" claim must be rewritten to the H1/H2
439	  % decomposition before this is re-attempted. See
440	  % informal/tensorObj_restrict_iso.md and task_results for the corrected route.
441	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
442	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
443	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
444	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
445	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
446	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
447	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
448	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
449	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
450	  isomorphism}
451	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
452	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
453	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
454	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
455	  therefore extension of scalars along
456	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
457	
458	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
459	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
460	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
461	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
462	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
463	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
464	  the abstract pullback functor.
465	
466	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
467	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
468	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
469	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
470	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
471	   \;\cong\;
472	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
473	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
474	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
475	  comparison
476	  \[
477	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
478	      \;\longrightarrow\;
479	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A

640	  % (def:scheme_modules_isinvertible), which carries the inverse existentially,
641	  % so no separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is required. Retained only
642	  % as a supplement on the geometric line-bundle subcategory.
643	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
644	  bundle. The dual
645	  \[
646	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
647	  \]
648	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
649	  isomorphism
650	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
651	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
652	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
653	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
654	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
655	\end{lemma}
656	
657	\begin{proof}
658	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
659	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
660	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
661	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
662	  The contraction map
663	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
664	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
665	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
666	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
667	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
668	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
669	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
670	\end{proof}

736	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
737	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
738	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
739	  % NOTE: no Lean declaration yet. NOT blocked on lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso:
740	  % pullback preserves the structure sheaf and tensor products (and hence
741	  % tensor-invertibility) by the standard pullback-tensor compatibility of
742	  % O-modules (Stacks lemma-tensor-product-pullback / lemma-pullback-invertible),
743	  % independently of the open-immersion restriction-compatibility iso.
744	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
745	  functor
746	  \[
747	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
748	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
749	  \]
750	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
751	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
752	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
753	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
754	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
755	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
756	  canonical isomorphism
757	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
758	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
759	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
760	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
761	\end{lemma}
762	
763	\begin{proof}
764	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
765	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
766	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
767	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
768	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
769	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
770	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
771	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
772	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
773	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
774	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
775	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
776	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
777	  same cover.
778	\end{proof}

780	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
781	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
782	
783	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
784	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
785	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
786	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
787	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
788	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
789	design template. The inverse of an invertible object is carried by the predicate
790	itself, so neither the open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
791	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a separate tensor-inverse lemma is on the
792	critical path.
793	
794	\begin{definition}[\(\otimes\)-invertibility of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
795	  \label{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
796	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible}
797	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
798	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
799	  % modules), Definition 01CS (definition-invertible) + Lemma 0B8K
800	  % (lemma-invertible, the "$\exists\,\mathcal{N}$" characterisation)
801	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4046-L4079).
802	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
803	  % "\begin{definition}
804	  % \label{definition-invertible}
805	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. An
806	  % {\it invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module} is a sheaf
807	  % of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{L}$ such that
808	  % the functor
809	  % $$
810	  % \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \longrightarrow \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X),\quad
811	  % \mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
812	  % $$
813	  % is an equivalence of categories. We say that $\mathcal{L}$ is
814	  % {\it trivial} if it is isomorphic as an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
815	  % to $\mathcal{O}_X$.
816	  % \end{definition}
817	  % [\dots]
818	  % \begin{lemma}
819	  % \label{lemma-invertible}
820	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Let $\mathcal{L}$
821	  % be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Equivalent are
822	  % \begin{enumerate}
823	  % \item $\mathcal{L}$ is invertible, and
824	  % \item there exists an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{N}$
825	  % such that
826	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X$.
827	  % \end{enumerate}
828	  % In this case $\mathcal{L}$ is locally a direct summand of a finite free
829	  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-module and the module $\mathcal{N}$ in (2) is isomorphic to
830	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$.
831	  % \end{lemma}"
832	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
833	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CS and Lemma~0B8K. An invertible
834	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is one for which \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
835	  (-)\) is an equivalence; equivalently there exists \(\mathcal{N}\) with
836	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), and
837	  then \(\mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
838	  \mathcal{O}_X)\).}
839	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). We say \(M\)

840	  is \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertible} when it admits a tensor inverse: there is an
841	  object \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
842	  \[
843	    M \otimes_X N \;\cong\;
844	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
845	      \;=\; \mathcal{O}_X ,
846	  \]
847	  i.e.\ \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M) :\equiv \exists\, N,\
848	  \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong
849	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)\), with
850	  \(\otimes_X\) the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The
851	  predicate is a \emph{proposition} carrying its inverse witness existentially;
852	  consequently the dual/inverse needed by the group law is definitional and no
853	  separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is consumed downstream. This is the
854	  scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\), transported
855	  one categorical level up to \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with the substrate
856	  \(\otimes_X\).
857	\end{definition}

859	\begin{lemma}[The commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units]
860	  \label{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}
861	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}
862	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
863	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
864	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
865	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
866	  % modules), Definition 01CX (definition-pic) + Lemma lemma-constructions-
867	  % invertible (tensor of invertibles is invertible; dual is the inverse)
868	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200-L4213 and L4350-L4357).
869	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
870	  % "\begin{lemma}
871	  % \label{lemma-constructions-invertible}
872	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
873	  % \begin{enumerate}
874	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$, $\mathcal{N}$ are invertible
875	  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, then so is
876	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N}$.
877	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
878	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
879	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
880	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
881	  % is an isomorphism.
882	  % \end{enumerate}
883	  % \end{lemma}
884	  % [\dots]
885	  % \begin{definition}
886	  % \label{definition-pic}
887	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
888	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
889	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
890	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
891	  % corresponding to tensor product.
892	  % \end{definition}"
893	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
894	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CX and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
895	  invertible}. The isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
896	  form an abelian group \(\Pic(X)\) under \(\otimes\), with unit
897	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and inverse the dual; the tensor product of invertibles is
898	  invertible and the dual is a tensor inverse.}
899	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of objects of
900	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) under the substrate tensor product
901	  \(\otimes_X\) form a commutative monoid: multiplication is
902	  \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\), well-defined on isomorphism classes
903	  because \(\otimes_X\) is a bifunctor
904	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, which transports
905	  \(M \cong M'\) to \(M \otimes_X N \cong M' \otimes_X N\) and symmetrically);
906	  the unit is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and associativity, unitality and
907	  commutativity are the coherence isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
908	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, read as
909	  equalities of iso-classes. An element \([M]\) is a \emph{unit} of this monoid
910	  iff \(M\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
911	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}): a tensor inverse \(N\) with
912	  \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) is exactly a multiplicative inverse of
913	  \([M]\). Hence the group of units of this commutative monoid is the abelian
914	  group of iso-classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules ---
915	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR). This mirrors Mathlib's
916	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\)
917	  and the transported \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\), used here as the design
918	  template; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at
919	  the pinned commit.
920	\end{lemma}
921	
922	\begin{proof}
923	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
924	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
925	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
926	  Well-definedness of \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\) on iso-classes is the
927	  bifunctoriality of \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}. The
928	  commutative-monoid axioms are propositions about iso-classes and are
929	  discharged by the coherence isomorphisms: associativity by
930	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unit laws by \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso},
931	  commutativity by \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}; no pentagon, triangle or
932	  hexagon identity is invoked, since each axiom asserts only the
933	  \emph{existence} of an isomorphism. By definition of a unit in a commutative
934	  monoid, \([M]\) is invertible iff there is \([N]\) with
935	  \([M \otimes_X N] = [\mathcal{O}_X]\), i.e.\ iff \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
936	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). Therefore the units subgroup is
937	  precisely the iso-classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible modules, and it is an
938	  abelian group --- the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR). The whole
939	  assembly follows the Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\)
940	  template.
941	\end{proof}

950	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
951	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.

953	\begin{theorem}

967	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
968	
969	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
970	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
971	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
972	  % groups)
973	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
974	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
975	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
976	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
977	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
978	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
979	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
980	  % \end{dfn}
981	  %  [\dots]
982	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
983	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
984	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
985	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
986	  % topologies  by
987	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
988	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
989	  %  \end{dfn}"
990	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
991	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
992	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
993	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
994	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
995	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
996	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
997	  \[
998	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
999	  \]
1000	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
1001	  abelian-group structure with addition
1002	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
1003	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
1004	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
1005	  inverse
1006	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
1007	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
1008	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1009	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
1010	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
1011	  map
1012	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
1013	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
1014	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1015	\end{theorem}
1016	
1017	\begin{proof}
1018	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1019	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
1020	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1021	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
1022	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
1023	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
1024	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
1025	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
1026	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
1027	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
1028	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
1029	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
1030	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
1031	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
1032	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
1033	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
1034	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
1035	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
1036	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
1037	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1038	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
1039	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
1040	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
1041	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
1042	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
1043	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
1044	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
1045	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
1046	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
1047	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
1048	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
1049	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
1050	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
1051	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
1052	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
1053	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
1054	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
1055	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
1056	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
1057	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
1058	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
1059	
1060	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
1061	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
1062	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
1063	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
1064	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
1065	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
1066	
1067	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
1068	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
1069	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
1070	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
1071	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
1072	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
1073	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
1074	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
1075	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
1076	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1077	\end{proof}

155	(which carries the inverse existentially) these assemble the commutative monoid
156	of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units group
157	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The project does \emph{not} require a
158	global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance on all quasi-coherent
159	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure:
160	monoidal coherence beyond these three isomorphisms is never consumed by a group

162	open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
163	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is \emph{not} on this critical path and is
164	retained only as an optional supplement.
165	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
166	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
167	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
168	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
169	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
170	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
171	independently of any upstream timing.

173	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
174	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
175	
176	\begin{definition}
177	
178	\leanok
179	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
180	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
181	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
182	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
183	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
184	  \[
185	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
186	  \]
187	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
188	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
189	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
190	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
191	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
192	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
193	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
194	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
195	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
196	  canonical compatibility
197	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
198	     \;\cong\;
199	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
200	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
201	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
202	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
203	
204	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
205	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
206	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
207	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
208	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
209	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
210	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
211	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
212	\end{definition}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}
215	
216	\leanok
217	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
218	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
219	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
220	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
221	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
222	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
223	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
224	  \[
225	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
226	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
227	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
228	  \]
229	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
230	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
231	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
232	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
233	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
234	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
235	  \[
236	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
237	    \qquad
238	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
239	  \]
240	  the associator
241	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
242	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
243	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
244	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they have no current Lean
245	  declaration and are off the critical path, recorded in
246	  \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
247	\end{lemma}
248	
249	\begin{proof}
250	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
251	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
252	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
253	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
254	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
255	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
256	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
257	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
258	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
259	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
260	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
261	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
262	\end{proof}
263	
264	\begin{remark}
265	[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
266	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
267	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
268	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
269	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
270	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
271	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
272	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
273	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
274	  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
275	  depend on it.
276	
277	  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
278	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
279	  classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
280	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
281	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
282	  coherence data of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
283	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never consumed by the group
284	  law. Conversely, building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all
285	  quasi-coherent modules would route through the localization-monoidal
286	  transport \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
287	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
288	  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
289	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
290	  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
291	  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
292	  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
293	  buys coherence the group law never uses.
294	
295	  The group law needs only the four existence-of-iso lemmas of
296	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}, each assembled from the
297	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. We
298	  therefore record the global monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the
299	  critical path} and build the group law directly on the line-bundle
300	  subcategory. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
301	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
302	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
303	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) over
304	  a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and no
305	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
306	\end{remark}
307	
308	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
309	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
310	
311	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
312	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
313	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
314	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
315	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section first isolates the one
316	genuinely-hard ingredient --- the restriction-compatibility
317	isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and then records the
318	\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
319	unit, commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law
320	consumes, each assembled from that ingredient on a common trivialising
321	cover, together with their restriction to the
322	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype and the interaction with the
323	pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). These four facts (\emph{not} a
324	\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance) are exactly the group-law data; cf.\

120	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)

122	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
123	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
124	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
125	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
126	
127	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
128	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
129	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
130	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
131	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
132	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
133	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
134	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
135	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
136	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
137	tensor-product operation
138	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
139	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
140	
141	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
142	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
143	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
144	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
145	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
146	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The data this operation must supply for the group law are the monoidal
147	\emph{coherence isomorphisms} of \(\otimes_X\) --- the associator
148	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), the left/right unitors
149	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}) and the braiding
150	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) --- each obtained by the cheap pattern
151	\(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) applied to the corresponding presheaf-level
152	isomorphism on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\), with no recourse to the
153	abstract \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\) adjoint. Together with the
154	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
155	(which carries the inverse existentially) these assemble the commutative monoid
156	of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units group
157	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The project does \emph{not} require a
158	global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance on all quasi-coherent
159	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure:
160	monoidal coherence beyond these three isomorphisms is never consumed by a group
161	law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}). The
162	open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
163	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is \emph{not} on this critical path and is
164	retained only as an optional supplement.
165	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
166	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
167	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
168	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
169	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
170	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
171	independently of any upstream timing.

173	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
174	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
175	
176	\begin{definition}
177	
178	\leanok
179	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
180	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
181	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
182	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
183	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
184	  \[
185	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
186	  \]
187	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
188	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
189	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
190	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
191	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
192	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
193	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
194	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
195	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
196	  canonical compatibility
197	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
198	     \;\cong\;
199	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
200	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
201	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
202	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
203	
204	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
205	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
206	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
207	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
208	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
209	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
210	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
211	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
212	\end{definition}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}
215	
216	\leanok
217	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
218	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
219	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
220	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
221	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
222	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
223	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
224	  \[
225	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
226	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
227	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
228	  \]
229	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
230	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
231	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
232	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
233	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
234	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
235	  \[
236	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
237	    \qquad
238	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
239	  \]
240	  the associator
241	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
242	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
243	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
244	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they have no current Lean
245	  declaration and are off the critical path, recorded in
246	  \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
247	\end{lemma}
248	
249	\begin{proof}
250	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
251	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
252	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
253	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
254	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
255	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
256	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
257	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
258	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
259	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
260	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
261	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
262	\end{proof}
263	
264	\begin{remark}
265	[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
266	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
267	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
268	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
269	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
270	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
271	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
272	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
273	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
274	  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
275	  depend on it.
276	
277	  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
278	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
279	  classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
280	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
281	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
282	  coherence data of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
283	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never consumed by the group
284	  law. Conversely, building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all
285	  quasi-coherent modules would route through the localization-monoidal
286	  transport \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
287	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
288	  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
289	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
290	  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
291	  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
292	  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
293	  buys coherence the group law never uses.
294	
295	  The group law needs only the four existence-of-iso lemmas of
296	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}, each assembled from the
297	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. We
298	  therefore record the global monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the
299	  critical path} and build the group law directly on the line-bundle
300	  subcategory. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
301	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
302	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
303	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) over
304	  a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and no
305	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
306	\end{remark}

308	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
309	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
310	
311	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
312	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
313	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
314	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
315	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section first isolates the one
316	genuinely-hard ingredient --- the restriction-compatibility
317	isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and then records the
318	\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
319	unit, commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law
320	consumes, each assembled from that ingredient on a common trivialising
321	cover, together with their restriction to the
322	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype and the interaction with the
323	pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). These four facts (\emph{not} a
324	\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance) are exactly the group-law data; cf.\
325	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
326	
327	\begin{lemma}
328	
329	\leanok
330	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
331	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
332	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
333	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
334	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
335	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
336	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
337	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
338	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
339	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and

340	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
341	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
342	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
343	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
344	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
345	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
346	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
347	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
348	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
349	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
350	  \[
351	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
352	      \;\;:=\;\;
353	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
354	  \]
355	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
356	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
357	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
358	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
359	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
360	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
361	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
362	\end{lemma}
363	
364	\begin{proof}
365	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
366	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
367	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
368	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
369	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
370	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
371	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
372	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
373	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
374	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
375	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
376	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
377	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
378	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
379	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
380	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
381	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
382	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
383	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
384	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
385	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
386	
387	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
388	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
389	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
390	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
391	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
392	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
393	\end{proof}
394	
395	\begin{lemma}
396	
397	\leanok
398	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
399	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
400	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
401	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
402	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
403	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
404	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
405	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
406	  morphism
407	  \[
408	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
409	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
410	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
411	  \]
412	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
413	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
414	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
415	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
416	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
417	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products. The intended Lean
418	  target is
419	  \(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\).
420	\end{lemma}

423	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}

425	  % "sectionwise unfolding of PresheafOfModules.pullback varphi (~30--60 lines)"
426	  % does NOT exist: that functor is the opaque abstract left adjoint of

428	  % the opacity, then assumes an unfolding anyway). The genuine residual needs

430	  % scale): (H1) a PRESHEAF-level iso `pushforward beta ~= pullback varphi` via
431	  % Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq from a presheaf-level pushforward adjunction
432	  % (needs presheaf pushforwardNatTrans + pushforwardCongr; only SHEAF versions

434	  % f.appIso (upgrade the file's lax `restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal` to strong;

436	  % monoidal"). Steps 1--2 (restrictFunctorIsoPullback, sheafificationCompPullback,
437	  % strip outer sheafification via mapIso) ARE formalized and GREEN. Step 3 and
438	  % the closing "30--60 line helper" claim must be rewritten to the H1/H2
439	  % decomposition before this is re-attempted. See
440	  % informal/tensorObj_restrict_iso.md and task_results for the corrected route.
441	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
442	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
443	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
444	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
445	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
446	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
447	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
448	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
449	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
450	  isomorphism}

454	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
455	  therefore extension of scalars along

458	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
459	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
460	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
461	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
462	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
463	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for

471	   \;\cong\;
472	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
473	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
474	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
475	  comparison
476	  \[
477	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
478	      \;\longrightarrow\;
479	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
480	      \;\otimes\;
481	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
482	  \]
483	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).

486	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise description of the abstract functor

502	\end{proof}

504	\begin{lemma}

506	\leanok
507	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
508	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}

510	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
511	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
512	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
513	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
514	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
515	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
516	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
517	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
518	\end{lemma}
519	
520	\begin{proof}
521	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
522	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
523	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
524	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
525	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
526	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
527	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
528	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
529	\end{proof}
530	
531	\begin{lemma}
532	[Associator for $\otimes_X$]
533	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
534	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
535	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
536	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
537	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. There is a natural isomorphism
538	  \[
539	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
540	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
541	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
542	  \]
543	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). No line-bundle, local-freeness or
544	  flatness hypothesis is required.
545	\end{lemma}
546	
547	\begin{proof}
548	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
549	  % NOTE: unlike the unitors and braiding, the associator nests a sheafification
550	  % inside the presheaf tensor on BOTH sides:
551	  %   tensorObj (tensorObj M N) P
552	  %     = sheafification(ptensor(sheafification(ptensor M.val N.val).val, P.val)).
553	  % Transporting the presheaf associator alpha therefore needs, in addition to
554	  % sheafification.mapIso(alpha), the "sheafification--tensor absorption" iso
555	  %   sheafification(ptensor(sheafification(A).val, B)) ~= sheafification(ptensor(A,B))
556	  % (and its mirror), i.e. the whiskering-stability of the sheafification
557	  % localizer for an arbitrary module --- the SAME stability that
558	  % rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path flags as routing through the absent
559	  % MonoidalClosed(PresheafOfModules R). The associator is thus NOT the bare

560	  % ~15-LOC mapIso pattern of the unitors/braiding; the absorption iso must be
561	  % supplied first. Flagged for the plan agent (see the blueprint-writer report's
562	  % Strategy-modifying findings).
563	  The substrate \(\otimes_X\) is, by \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}, the
564	  sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
565	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\). The presheaf category
566	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) is monoidal
567	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}), so it carries
568	  an associator natural isomorphism \(\alpha\) at the presheaf level. Applying
569	  the sheafification functor to \(\alpha\) --- the cheap pattern
570	  \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\alpha)\), exactly the shape of the existing
571	  structure-sheaf-unit comparison and of \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoOfIso}\) --- and
572	  composing with the comparison identifying iterated sheafified tensor products
573	  with the sheafification of the iterated presheaf tensor product yields the
574	  asserted isomorphism. The construction uses only the monoidal coherence data
575	  already present on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\); it invokes neither
576	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\) nor any open-immersion
577	  restriction-compatibility.
578	\end{proof}

580	\begin{lemma}
581	[Left and right unitors for $\otimes_X$]
582	  \label{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
583	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_left_unitor, AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_right_unitor}
584	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
585	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) an
586	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. With
587	  \(\mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) the
588	  structure-sheaf unit object, there are natural isomorphisms
589	  \[
590	    \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M,
591	    \qquad
592	    M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M .
593	  \]
594	\end{lemma}
595	
596	\begin{proof}
597	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
598	  The presheaf monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) supplies
599	  the left and right unitors \(\lambda, \rho\) at the presheaf level. Their
600	  sheafifications \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\lambda)\),
601	  \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\rho)\), composed with the sheafification
602	  counit identifying the sheafified structure-sheaf presheaf with
603	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (the step already used in the structure-sheaf-unit
604	  comparison \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_unit\_iso}\) in the substrate file), give the
605	  two isomorphisms. As with the associator the route is the cheap
606	  \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern and uses no abstract pullback.
607	\end{proof}

610	[Braiding for $\otimes_X$]
611	  \label{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
612	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_braiding}
613	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}

615	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. There is a natural isomorphism
616	  \[

618	  \]
619	\end{lemma}

621	\begin{proof}
622	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}

624	  braiding \(\beta\) at the presheaf level
625	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}). Its
